@graph.name: Pip names himself from his mispronunciation of Philip Pirrip
@graph.source_quote: My infant tongue could make of both names nothing longer or more explicit than Pip. So, I called myself Pip, and came to be called Pip.

@graph.name: Pip explains knowledge of his parents through his sister and tombstones
@graph.source_quote: As I never saw my father or my mother, and never saw any likeness of either of them, my first fancies regarding what they were like were unreasonably derived from their tombstones.

@graph.name: Pip forms childish imaginary portraits of his parents from inscriptions
@graph.source_quote: The shape of the letters on my father’s, gave me an odd idea that he was a square, stout, dark man, with curly black hair.

@graph.name: Pip imagines his dead brothers’ lives from their small gravestones
@graph.source_quote: I am indebted for a belief I religiously entertained that they had all been born on their backs with their hands in their trousers-pockets.

@graph.name: Pip situates his childhood in the marsh country and recalls a vivid afternoon
@graph.source_quote: Ours was the marsh country, down by the river, within, as the river wound, twenty miles of the sea.
@graph.time: a memorable raw afternoon towards evening
@graph.location: marsh country by the river, near the sea

@graph.name: Pip recognizes the graveyard, surrounding landscape, and his own identity
@graph.source_quote: I found out for certain that this bleak place overgrown with nettles was the churchyard... and that the small bundle of shivers... was Pip.
@graph.location: churchyard and adjacent marshes by the river and sea

@graph.name: A terrifying convict suddenly confronts Pip in the graveyard
@graph.source_quote: “Hold your noise!” cried a terrible voice, as a man started up from among the graves... “Keep still, you little devil, or I’ll cut your throat!”
@graph.time: immediately after Pip’s fearful recognition of the setting

@graph.name: Pip pleads for his life, revealing his terror before the convict
@graph.source_quote: “Oh! Don’t cut my throat, sir,” I pleaded in terror. “Pray don’t do it, sir.”

@graph.name: Convict forces Pip to reveal where he lives
@graph.source_quote: “Show us where you live,” said the man. “Pint out the place!”

@graph.name: Convict searches Pip and eats his bread
@graph.source_quote: turned me upside down, and emptied my pockets... while he ate the bread ravenously.
@graph.time: Immediately after Pip points out the village

@graph.name: Pip trembles while observing the man's roughness and hunger
@graph.source_quote: I was seated on a high tombstone, trembling while he ate the bread ravenously.
@graph.time: During and after the man's search and eating
@graph.location: On a high tombstone in the churchyard

@graph.name: Man comments on Pip’s physical appearance
@graph.source_quote: “You young dog,” said the man, licking his lips, “what fat cheeks you ha’ got.”

@graph.name: The man threatens to eat the child
@graph.source_quote: “Darn me if I couldn’t eat ’em,” said the man, with a threatening shake of his head...

@graph.name: Pip clings to the tombstone to steady himself and suppress tears
@graph.source_quote: I earnestly expressed my hope that he wouldn’t, and held tighter to the tombstone...

@graph.name: The man demands to know where Pip’s mother is
@graph.source_quote: “Now lookee here!” said the man. “Where’s your mother?”

@graph.name: Pip reveals his mother is buried in the churchyard
@graph.source_quote: “There, sir!” I timidly explained. “Also Georgiana. That’s my mother.”

@graph.name: Stranger questions Pip about his parents and guardians
@graph.source_quote: “Who d’ye live with,—supposin’ you’re kindly let to live, which I han’t made up my mind about?”
@graph.location: Graveyard (implicit from context of parents' graves)

@graph.name: Man reacts to learning Joe is a blacksmith and glances at his leg
@graph.source_quote: “Blacksmith, eh?” said he. And looked down at his leg.

@graph.name: Convict intimidates Pip and threatens his life while demanding help
@graph.source_quote: “You get me a file… And you get me wittles… Or I’ll have your heart and liver out.”

@graph.name: Pip pleads for physical relief so he can comply
@graph.source_quote: “If you would kindly please to let me keep upright, sir, perhaps I shouldn’t be sick, and perhaps I could attend more.”

@graph.name: Convict threatens Pip into bringing tools and food
@graph.source_quote: “You bring me, to-morrow morning early, that file and them wittles... and you shall be let to live.”

@graph.name: Pip promises to bring the file and food
@graph.source_quote: I said that I would get him the file, and I would get him what broken bits of food I could...
@graph.time: Immediately after the threat; for early next morning

@graph.name: The stranger warns Pip to remember his promise and sends him home
@graph.source_quote: “Now,” he pursued, “you remember what you’ve undertook, and you remember that young man, and you get home!”
@graph.time: immediately following Pip's encounter with the man

@graph.name: The man limps away across the graveyard toward the river, shivering and hugging himself
@graph.source_quote: He hugged his shuddering body in both his arms… and limped towards the low church wall.
@graph.location: from the churchyard towards the river across the marshes

@graph.name: Pip runs home while watching the man’s retreat and imagining the dead grasping at him
@graph.source_quote: He looked in my young eyes as if he were eluding the hands of the dead people, stretching up cautiously out of their graves, to get a twist upon his ankle and pull him in.
@graph.time: as the man climbs the wall and moves away
@graph.location: churchyard and view over the marshes

@graph.name: The landscape of marshes, river, and sky is described as stark and ominous
@graph.source_quote: The marshes were just a long black horizontal line then… and the sky was just a row of long angry red lines and dense black lines intermixed.
@graph.time: as Pip stops to look after the man

@graph.name: Pip observes a limping man moving toward a distant gibbet
@graph.source_quote: The man was limping on towards this latter, as if he were the pirate come to life, and come down, and going back to hook himself up again.
@graph.location: edge of the river near the beacon and gibbet

@graph.name: Pip imagines the limping man as a resurrected pirate and is terrified
@graph.source_quote: It gave me a terrible turn when I thought so; and as I saw the cattle lifting their heads to gaze after him, I wondered whether they thought so too.
@graph.time: immediately after observing the limping man

@graph.name: Pip, unable to see the ‘horrible young man,’ flees home in renewed fear
@graph.source_quote: I looked all round for the horrible young man, and could see no signs of him. But now I was frightened again, and ran home without stopping.
@graph.time: immediately after searching for the horrible young man

@graph.entity_name: Infant inability to pronounce his full names

Childish tendency to derive character and appearance from written words and shapes
Lack of realistic images in an era before photographs
Childlike anthropomorphizing of the stones’ form into imagined postures of the brothers
Desire to make sense of death and family through fanciful images
Desire to frame his earliest sense of the world and identity
Geographical context of Pip’s upbringing near river and sea
marsh country by the river, near the sea
Pip is alone in a bleak, frightening environment and seeking to understand where he is and who he is
The narrator retrospectively clarifies the setting and his family situation to the reader
churchyard and adjacent marshes by the river and sea
The escaped convict needs to control and intimidate Pip to prevent noise and discovery
The author introduces a menacing figure to catalyze the plot and establish gothic tension
The convict’s violent threat and physical seizure of Pip provoke instinctive fear and a plea for mercy
The man needs information about nearby habitations, likely for food or shelter
The man's fugitive, desperate condition drives him to threaten a child
The man is hungry and desperate for food
Physical domination is used to control Pip and secure resources
The man's sudden, violent handling terrifies the child
The graveyard and tombstone setting heighten Pip’s sense of vulnerability
On a high tombstone in the churchyard
The man’s hunger makes him focus on Pip’s apparent health and “fat cheeks”
The comment underscores the contrast between the starving convict and the relatively well-fed child
The man wants to intimidate the child into obedience
The man is a desperate escaped convict exerting power through fear
Pip is frightened by the man’s threats and seeks physical and emotional support
The man is probing Pip’s family situation and presence in the churchyard
Pip answers the man’s question literally by pointing to the grave
Pip’s orphaned status is revealed and emphasized through the tombstone
The man needs to know who Pip belongs to and whether he can threaten or use him safely
The man is evaluating Pip's social ties and possible help or retaliation from adults
Introducing Pip’s guardians and Joe as key figures in his life
Graveyard (implicit from context of parents' graves)
Information that Joe Gargery is a blacksmith
A blacksmith is directly relevant to his leg, suggesting irons, chains, or escape needs
The man is likely a convict associating blacksmithing with removing restraints
Foreshadowing future involvement of Joe’s skills with the man’s condition
Convict needs a file to remove his leg-iron and food to survive
Pip is a vulnerable child alone in a desolate churchyard
The convict uses physical domination (tilting Pip back) to enhance fear and compliance
Pip feels intense fear, giddiness, and physical discomfort from being tilted back
Pip believes that being allowed to stand upright will help him obey the convict's demands
Convict needs a file and food to aid his escape and survival
Use of terrifying threats and an invented ‘young man’ to ensure the boy’s obedience
Pip’s fear and vulnerability as a child alone with a desperate criminal
Pip is terrified by threats of violent death and the mysterious ‘young man’
Power imbalance between a small boy and a violent adult convict
The man depends on Pip to bring him food and a file as promised
Threat of the 'young man' is used to intimidate Pip into compliance
He needs to return to hiding on the marshes near the river
His physical suffering from cold, wet, and injury drives his movements
from the churchyard towards the river across the marshes
Pip is frightened by the man and by the atmosphere of the graveyard
Pip’s childish imagination transforms the graves into grasping hands
The bleak marsh landscape heightens his sense of dread
churchyard and view over the marshes
Pip pauses to watch the man and takes in the surrounding scene
The bleak lines of marsh, river, and sky underscore the loneliness and severity of the situation
Pip is scanning the bleak landscape after his encounter with the convict
The limping man’s movement toward the gibbet visually connects him with the executed pirate
edge of the river near the beacon and gibbet
Childlike imagination connects the limping man with the pirate’s chains on the gibbet
Recent exposure to fear and threats on the marshes heightens Pip’s terror
Pip cannot locate the imagined ‘horrible young man’ and feels unsafe on the marshes
Prior threat about the ‘young man’ makes Pip hyper-vigilant and fearful

@graph.mechanism: Realizing he is in the churchyard and that the shivering child is Pip enables the convict to stop him, physically handle him, and search his pockets for food.
His act of limping toward the low church wall allows the observer to see and interpret his motion as that of the pirate coming back to hook himself up again.
Being robbed and threatened increases the narrator’s fear, leading him to cling more tightly to the tombstone and plead more earnestly.
The boy’s terrified plea prompts the man to pause from threatening him and instead question him, shifting the interaction from immediate violence to interrogation.
The man’s observation of the boy’s fat cheeks leads directly to his remark that he could eat them, expressing a mock-threatening reaction to what he has just noticed.
The man’s noticing Pip’s plump cheeks makes him focus on the boy and then question him, prompting him to ask where Pip’s mother is.
The threat and demand for a file and food sets up Pip’s obligation; reminding him of his undertaking and sending him home enables him to fulfill the earlier coerced promise.
The man’s violent threat terrifies the narrator, making him obey, stay seated, and tremble while the man eats the bread.
The threats about getting a file and food set up the obligation and fear that allow him to demand Pip remember the undertaking and go home to fulfill it.

The convict’s threat to Pip’s life plants intense fear, which frames how Pip perceives him and his situation; this heightened fear enables Pip’s vivid, morbid interpretation of the convict’s glance as if escaping grasping dead hands.
Recognizing that the frightening, threatening figure is addressing him personally as Pip in the churchyard confirms the danger, triggering his terrified plea not to have his throat cut.
The threat of death conditional on bringing tools and food motivates Pip’s terrified reaction and departure; the demand and threat enable his decision to leave and fetch what was ordered.
The man’s aggressive remark sets a threatening tone that leads into his interrogation, prompting him to demand where the mother is.
His fear while watching the man ravenously eat the bread leads him to plead more earnestly and cling tighter to the tombstone for safety.
The threatening reminder of Pip’s promise and the young man compels him, out of fear, to leave immediately and head toward the church wall/home.
Pip’s terrified pleading likely softens the convict’s immediate threat, enabling him to spare Pip and instead sit and eat the bread while Pip trembles nearby.
The threat of violence (heart and liver out) motivates the conditional promise of survival if the boy brings the file and food, driving the later statement that he will be let live if he complies.
Recognizing Pip and the churchyard gives the man enough information and confidence to order Pip to reveal where he lives, advancing his interrogation and demands.
Because he has never seen his parents and instead imagines them from their tombstones, the child infers his father’s physical appearance from the shape of the letters on the stone.

His limping movement toward the churchyard wall, hugging his shuddering body, positions him near the graves and makes it plausible for the narrator to interpret his behavior as if he were evading dead hands pulling at his ankle.
The narrator’s plea and clinging to the tombstone prompt the threatening man to reconsider immediate harm and instead question where the narrator lives.
The man’s violent threat and seizure of the boy coerce compliance, allowing him to physically grab the boy, turn him upside down, and take the bread to eat.

Pip’s lack of memories of his parents and reliance on their tombstones sets the context for his identity and perception, enabling the later realization that the bleak churchyard and the shivering child are himself.
The man’s direct question about the mother prompts the narrator to point her out and explain, leading to the specific response identifying Georgiana as his mother.
The convict’s plea about needing to stay upright to not be sick elicits pity and compliance from Pip, prompting him to promise to bring the file and food.
The convict’s threatening attention to his leg, where his iron is fixed, implies he needs a file to remove it, prompting Pip to promise to bring a file and food.
The convict’s violent threats intimidate Pip, discouraging him from collapsing or being sick so he can obey and pay attention; fear suppresses his impulse to be ill or unresponsive.
The boy’s terrified plea signals submission and reduces resistance, enabling the assailant to handle him easily, turn him upside down, and rob him while eating the bread.
Pip’s plea and apparent vulnerability help move the convict to bargain rather than simply threaten, enabling the specific conditional demand and promise about bringing the file and food.
The speaker’s promise of help (file and food) reassures the man and morally compels him to leave rather than harm the child, enabling him to look away as if escaping the dead’s grasp.
Having taken the boy’s food and inspected him closely, the man is positioned to notice and comment on his physical appearance, leading to the remark about his fat cheeks.
The man’s explicit threat to cut the boy’s throat directly induces the boy’s terrified plea for mercy in response.
The demand to reveal his home escalates the man’s hostile, predatory attitude, making him express a desire to eat them and shake his head threateningly.
The question about who Pip lives with leads the convict to threaten him into bringing a file and food, using fear to coerce compliance.

The convict’s initial threat and question about whether Pip will be allowed to live establishes coercion, directly leading to his conditional promise that Pip will be spared if he brings the file and food.
The man’s initial violent threat subdues and intimidates the child, prompting the man to continue coercing him by demanding he reveal where he lives.
The boy’s bread alleviates the man’s hunger, prompting him to say he could eat the boy’s hands and liver, turning his hunger into a specific, threatening remark.

The description of the marsh country situates the narrative setting in which the bleak, nettle-overgrown churchyard exists, enabling the later discovery and identification of Pip in that specific place.
Her frightening thought about the young man and imagining even the cattle shared it heightens her fear, which later leads her to become frightened again and run home without stopping.
Being turned upside down and having pockets emptied intimidates and physically manipulates the narrator, resulting in him ending up trembling and seated on the tombstone while the other eats.
The convict ravenously eating the bread highlights Pip’s well-fed appearance, prompting the man to notice and comment on his fat cheeks after satisfying his immediate hunger.
By turning him upside down and emptying his pockets, the man gains access to the bread, enabling him to eat and then threaten he could eat more.
The prisoner’s terrified pleading likely makes the men reconsider killing him and instead decide to extract information, leading them to demand he show where he lives.
The narrator’s resistance and tightening grip on the tombstone likely provoke the threatening character to escalate his intimidation and make harsher demands.
Pip’s terrified pleading and submission prompt the convict to spare him and instead threaten him into bringing a file and food as conditions for not harming him.
The threat and rough handling implied in questioning whether she’ll be ‘let to live’ causes physical distress, leading her to ask to stand upright so she won’t be sick and can pay attention.
The speaker’s promise to bring the file and food motivates the other character to sternly remind him of his commitment and send him home to fulfill it.
The man’s demand to see where he lives intimidates the narrator, leading him to sit fearfully on a tombstone and tremble while the man eats.
Realizing the boy’s connection to the blacksmith suggests access to tools and food, prompting the convict to threaten him to obtain a file and vittles.
Realizing Pip’s connection to a blacksmith leads the convict to coerce him into bringing a file and food, using the threat of death as leverage.
The man’s threatening comment about the boy’s fat cheeks implies potential harm, prompting the boy to respond by pleading and gripping the tombstone more tightly out of fear.
The man begins interrogating the child by asking about his mother, which initiates the questioning that leads to his follow-up question about who the child lives with.
The man’s haunted, fearful look suggests pursuit by dead people, instilling fear in the narrator, which leads him to look around anxiously, fail to find the man, become frightened, and run home.
The man’s threatening remark about being able to eat them sets a menacing tone that leads directly into his follow-up question about whether they’ll be allowed to live and whom they live with.
The promise of help (file and food) gives him motive and perhaps hope, enabling him to move away toward the wall rather than remain passive.
The narrator’s perception of the limping man as resembling the pirate directly triggers his terrifying thought, which in turn produces the emotional reaction described.
Lacking real memories or images of his parents, he uses tombstone engravings as his only visual reference, leading him to form an imaginative but mistaken belief about how they looked and were born.

The ominous description of the marshes and sky creates a threatening, uncanny atmosphere that facilitates the narrator’s fearful interpretation and emotional shock when imagining others sharing that perception.
The man demands to know where the boy’s mother is to ensure privacy/control before threatening him for a file and food; this prior inquiry helps him secure the boy’s full attention and compliance.
The man’s threatening remark about eating them frightens the narrator, prompting a verbal plea and a physical reaction of gripping the tombstone tighter.
The limping man’s approach, resembling the pirate, intensifies the narrator’s fear, prompting them to become frightened again and run home without stopping.
The authority figure’s focus on the boy (noticing him and his leg) prompts the boy’s request to remain upright, which he believes will help him avoid sickness and pay better attention.
The man’s demand to see where the speaker lives implies coercion and threat, which leads to physically overpowering the speaker, turning him upside down, and taking his possessions and food.
The convict’s threat of death if Pip does not bring a file and food directly motivates Pip’s promise to obtain them, making the demand and threat the cause of his decision.
The eerie, threatening appearance of the marshes and sky heightens the narrator’s sense of danger and vulnerability, enabling and intensifying his fear and prompting his decision to run home.
His eerie, death-evading manner of looking at the narrator prompts the narrator’s disturbing interpretation, which in turn triggers the emotional shock and wondering about the cattle’s thoughts.
The convict threatens violence if the boy does not bring a file and food, directly prompting the boy’s promise to get those items.
